entities.description: The abstract Middle English verse translation and adaptation represented by three divergent witnesses. It is not any one codex, witness spelling, scholarly edition, or reconstructed critical text.

rights.basis: The work and all three manuscript witnesses are medieval and centuries outside copyright term. This does not grant rights in modern photographs, catalogs, or editions.

entities.description: The late-antique Latin moral and educational work translated directly in the Northern Middle English version. The English translator's exact Latin exemplar is unidentified; Förster's use of Baehrens for reference numbering does not make that modern edition a medieval source.

localCopies.label: Sidney Sussex Special Collections access and digitization page

notes: The official Special Collections page lists complete digital copies of Sidney MSS 2, 79, 95, 109, and Ward B, but not MS 63; its separate image list also omits MS 63.

access.accessMethod: Ask the Archivist whether a complete color digital reproduction of the current physical MS 63 can be supplied. Specify covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all endleaves and blanks, every recto and verso, the separately sleeved parchment leaf, cancelled-leaf stubs and detached matter where present. Separately identify Cato at ff. 202vb–208rb and ask the repository to confirm the current foliation and complete-object inventory.
access.format: Repository-supplied photographs or digital files, subject to condition, quotation, and permission
notes: The official page says the Archivist may be able to satisfy photographic reproduction requests for suitable material. Ask separately whether supplied images may be redistributed in Git or used in commercial or print-on-demand publication.

rights.basis: The official page offers photographic reproduction enquiries but does not state an item-level open license or publication permission for supplied files.
rights.component: Any MS 63 photographs supplied by Sidney Sussex College
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms not publicly specified at item level
notes: Ask explicitly about Git redistribution, public viewer display, scholarly publication, and commercial or print-on-demand use before publishing supplied images.

access.accessMethod: External researchers are welcomed by appointment; contact the Archivist in writing at least one week in advance and bring identification.

entities.description: A paper Confessio Amantis codex with the Northern Cato translation added at ff. 202vb–208rb. This witness is one representation of the work, not its source manuscript or a chapter of a composite work.

holding.notes: No repository-owned exact item record was identified. The separately modeled Pearsall-Mooney scholarly catalog chapter and Sidney Special Collections page provide description and access routes without masquerading as a holding catalog URL.

entities.label: Sidney Sussex College MS 63

physicalDescription.extent: James reports 210 leaves; modern foliation extends through at least f. 211v. The Cato occupies ff. 202vb–208rb.
physicalDescription.format: Double columns, historically described as 43 lines per column
notes: Pearsall and Mooney describe one early-to-mid-fifteenth-century scribe writing both Confessio Amantis and Cato.
The modern locus ff. 202vb–208rb refines James's older 202b–208a description and Förster's 202b–208a notation.
DIMEV associates the manuscript language with eLALME 1.64, Ely; this witness-level scribal localization does not displace Förster's work-level Northern rhyme analysis.
physicalDescription.support: Paper, with a separately kept parchment replacement or flyleaf at the beginning

rights.basis: The official page offers photographic reproduction enquiries but does not state an item-level open license or publication permission for supplied files.
rights.component: Any MS 63 photographs supplied by Sidney Sussex College
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms not publicly specified at item level
notes: Ask explicitly about Git redistribution, public viewer display, scholarly publication, and commercial or print-on-demand use before publishing supplied images.

access.accessMethod: Request a complete color reproduction of the current physical MS. Rawl. G. 59: covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all parchment flyleaves, the iii later endleaves, every blank and every recto and verso of all 16 leaves, plus detached matter and edges where offered. Separately identify the encoded work as ff. 1r–11v, DIMEV's broader locus ff. 1–13, and the current catalog's inferred item ff. 1r–16v; ask the repository to confirm the current work boundaries.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century English paper codex containing the Latin Disticha Catonis with Middle English translation. Förster prints a strongly revised witness version.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Rawl. G. 59

physicalDescription.extent: iii later endleaves + 16 leaves. The current catalogue broadly infers the item at ff. 1r–16v; DIMEV gives ff. 1–13; Förster and CME milestones delimit the printed Middle English stream at ff. 1r–11v.
notes: The differing work loci describe different cataloging and edition boundaries and require repository confirmation before a requested facsimile is mapped.
The probable Dartford Priory association rests on 'Suster Emme Wynter' at f. 13r.
physicalDescription.support: Paper, with a pair of parchment flyleaves at each end

localCopies.label: Royal Danish Library manuscript access guide

notes: The current Alma record offers only physical reading-room delivery and no electronic service; the Western Manuscripts digital-collections query returned zero records. This does not prove that internal images do not exist.

access.accessMethod: Choose manuscript/letter and request the complete current Thott 306 folio object, not an excerpt: case or covers, boards, spine, pastedowns, all endleaves and blanks, and both sides of all 14 parchment leaves, plus detached matter and edges where offered. State that the work occupies ff. 1–14 and ask for an inventory of every delivered surface.
access.contact: Online form on the digitisation page; kb@kb.dk; +45 3347 4747
access.format: PDF 150 dpi, JPEG 72 dpi, or TIFF 300 ppi under current service options

localCopies.label: Royal Danish Library digitisation services and prices
Royal Danish Library copyright and use guidance

notes: Current foreign-user prices list PDF at DKK 10 per ordinary A4/A3 page or DKK 15 per difficult/valuable page, with a DKK 250 minimum and DKK 5,000 per-volume maximum for the latter category; JPEG is DKK 30 per file with a DKK 600 minimum; high-resolution hardcover imaging is DKK 425 per file including VAT for originals up to A1. The library determines feasibility and quotes the order.

The library reserves the right to make a purchased digitisation freely available later. Ask explicitly whether the delivered files may be redistributed in Git and used in commercial or print-on-demand publication.
If complete coverage cannot be supplied, request a written list of omitted surfaces and record the delivery as partial.

rights.basis: The underlying medieval material is public domain, but the current general digitisation and copyright pages do not grant an item-specific open license for a future delivered file set.
rights.component: Any Thott 306 digitisation supplied by Royal Danish Library
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and applicable Danish law
notes: Ask for written clarification of Git redistribution, public display, and commercial or print-on-demand reuse. The library may later make purchased digitisation available to other users, but that statement alone is not a license for the requester to redistribute the files.

access.accessMethod: Order the available manuscript through the library system for consultation in the Black Diamond research reading room under current special regulations.

dateStatements.label: production, current Royal Danish Library catalogue

entities.description: The third and textually best witness identified by Brusendorff. The complete 14-leaf parchment codex contains only the Latin Disticha and its Middle English verse rendering, with each Latin distich followed by the English paraphrase.
entities.label: Royal Danish Library Thott 306 folio

physicalDescription.extent: 14 leaves in two quires of eight and six leaves; the work occupies the complete manuscript, ff. 1–14.
physicalDescription.format: One bound volume in a bookbinder's case; Latin distich followed by Middle English paraphrase
notes: Brusendorff dates the hand to the close of the fourteenth century; the current Royal Danish Library record gives only the fifteenth century. Both attributed datings are retained.
The current record reports reading-room delivery and no electronic service.

rights.basis: The underlying medieval material is public domain, but the current general digitisation and copyright pages do not grant an item-specific open license for a future delivered file set.

next_steps: Request the current specialist manuscript description or dating rationale from Royal Danish Library.
Retain both attributed datings until a current paleographical assessment resolves the difference.
openQuestions.question: Should Thott 306 be dated to the close of the fourteenth century, as Brusendorff argued, or only broadly to the fifteenth century, as the current Royal Danish Library record states?

next_steps: Ask the Bodleian to confirm current foliation and identify the last Middle English line of DIMEV 6324.
Map all three boundary claims against a complete reproduction when obtained.
openQuestions.question: How do Förster's ff. 1r–11v transcription boundary, DIMEV's ff. 1–13 locus, and the current Bodleian record's inferred ff. 1r–16v item boundary correspond in the physical codex?

next_steps: Consult a modern critical study or edition of DIMEV 6324.
Do not convert Förster's or Brusendorff's qualitative judgments into direct-copy relations among the surviving codices without stemmatic evidence.
openQuestions.question: What stemmatic relationship does current scholarship support among Thott, Sidney, and Rawlinson?

next_steps: Request whole-object quotes and inventories from all three repositories.
Record each delivered file's exact terms and coverage before public redistribution.
Prefer the complete codex even when the work occupies only a portion, and retain exact work-to-folio mappings.
openQuestions.question: Can the three repositories supply complete color facsimiles, and under what Git, public-viewer, scholarly-publication, and commercial print-on-demand terms?

reviewNotes: The third Thott witness is absent from Förster and therefore from CME00083; it is included as work-lineage evidence, not as an encoded source.
Whole-manuscript facsimile coverage is the target for future downloads. The one verified public manuscript plate remains marked partial and is never substituted for the complete Thott codex.
The local source cache is gitignored. Four shared public support files are true hard links: DIMEV v1.0 and the three Bodleian forms or terms documents.

sourceLinks.accessMethod: Choose manuscript/letter and request the complete current Thott 306 folio object, not an excerpt: case or covers, boards, spine, pastedowns, all endleaves and blanks, and both sides of all 14 parchment leaves, plus detached matter and edges where offered. State that the work occupies ff. 1–14 and ask for an inventory of every delivered surface.

localCopies.label: Royal Danish Library digitisation services and prices
Royal Danish Library copyright and use guidance

notes: Current foreign-user prices list PDF at DKK 10 per ordinary A4/A3 page or DKK 15 per difficult/valuable page, with a DKK 250 minimum and DKK 5,000 per-volume maximum for the latter category; JPEG is DKK 30 per file with a DKK 600 minimum; high-resolution hardcover imaging is DKK 425 per file including VAT for originals up to A1. The library determines feasibility and quotes the order.

The library reserves the right to make a purchased digitisation freely available later. Ask explicitly whether the delivered files may be redistributed in Git and used in commercial or print-on-demand publication.
If complete coverage cannot be supplied, request a written list of omitted surfaces and record the delivery as partial.

lineage.summary: CME00083 is the checked-in copy of the University of Michigan encoding of Max Förster's complete 1906 article 'Eine nordenglische Cato-Version.' Förster prints two divergent manuscript versions on alternating pages: Sidney Sussex College MS 63 on even pages 4–54 and Bodleian MS. Rawl. G. 59 on odd pages 5–55, preceded by his German introduction on pages 1–3. Aage Brusendorff identified a third witness in Royal Danish Library Thott 306 folio, which he described as earlier and textually better, and printed selected passages plus one manuscript photograph in 1929. DIMEV 6324 now records all three witnesses. Complete scans of both historical print containers and the complete DIMEV v1.0 release are cached locally. No complete public facsimile was found for any of the three physical manuscripts on 2026-07-12. The only public manuscript image verified is Brusendorff's monochrome plate of the upper half of Thott fol. A2r; it is explicitly partial. Whole-object reproduction routes therefore ask for every present manuscript surface and separately map the work loci within the full codices.

catalogSummary.title: The Northern Version of Cato's Distichs

catalogingSubject.label: The Northern Version of Cato's Distichs
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous fourteenth-century Northern Middle English verse translation and adaptation of the Latin Disticha Catonis represented by the Thott, Sidney, and Rawlinson witnesses. It is one work with divergent witness versions, not a three-part compilation. It is distinct from the Latin source work, each physical codex, Förster's 1906 German study and two-witness edition, Brusendorff's 1929 Thott selections, U-M's electronic encoding, and the checked-in XML. CME00083 contains only Förster's Sidney and Rawlinson streams; the third Thott witness belongs to the wider work lineage but is not an encoded source.

contentParts.label: The Northern Version of Cato's Distichs

locators.value: BODY/DIV1/DIV2[2] Sidney and DIV2[3] Rawlinson; 297 LG and 1,265 L aggregate
Sidney pp. 4–54 even; Rawlinson pp. 5–55 odd
Förster/CME ff. 1r–11v; DIMEV ff. 1–13; current catalog item inferred ff. 1r–16v

notes: Förster judges Sidney closer to the original wording and Rawlinson strongly revised, while Brusendorff calls Thott earlier and better; no direct-copy stemma among the three surviving codices is asserted.
The Thott witness is not present in CME00083 and must not be implied to have been a source for U-M or Förster.
contentParts.summary: The complete anonymous Middle English moral and educational poem as transmitted through divergent witness versions; the witness streams are representations of this one work, not sequential constituent parts.

assertion.notes: A work-level estimate, not the production date of any one surviving copy.

dateStatements.display: Fourteenth century, on Förster's linguistic and rhyme analysis

assertion.notes: The broad normalized range preserves the unresolved dating conflict. Brusendorff calls Thott earlier than Sidney and Rawlinson, but the current catalog's broad fifteenth-century date does not independently prove exact priority.

dateStatements.display: Earliest surviving attestation window: late fourteenth to fifteenth century; Brusendorff called Thott 306 earlier, but its current broad dating overlaps the other witnesses

assertion.notes: Förster notes an earlier brief notice of Rawlinson G. 59 by Napier; the 1906 article is the first complete edition verified here.

extent.description: One verse work represented in two complete CME witness streams. Sidney contributes 153 LG and 654 L; Rawlinson contributes 144 LG and 611 L. The 297-group and 1,265-line totals are repository-encoding metrics across parallel versions. Förster's separate German introduction occupies printed pp. 1–3 and is edition framing, not another medieval work or intrinsic section.

formStatements.description: Paired-rhyming four-stress verse, with some witness-specific variation and six Sidney tail-rhyme stanzas whose originality Förster leaves unresolved.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The Latin Disticha is the related source work. Förster's German introduction and notes and his Latin references are edition-level languages and are not promoted to primary languages of the cataloged medieval work.
The work-level Northern localization and the Sidney witness's eLALME Ely scribal localization belong to different layers and are not contradictory claims about one event.
German is the language of Förster's introduction and notes, not the primary language of the cataloged medieval work; Latin belongs chiefly to the related source work and witness framing.
Förster's 677 encoded italics preserve edition typography, not the manuscripts' original abbreviation marks. Whole-codex images are required before reconstructing those source features.
The Thott witness is a related source for the work record but not a source witness for the CME/Förster encoding.

next_steps: Request the library's current detailed manuscript description or paleographical assessment.
Keep both datings attributed until resolved.
openQuestions.question: Can a current specialist description resolve Brusendorff's close-fourteenth-century dating of Thott 306 against the Royal Danish Library's current fifteenth-century record?

next_steps: Consult a current critical study of DIMEV 6324 and the three-witness tradition.
Do not convert witness production dates into a precise composition date.
openQuestions.question: What composition or translation date does current scholarship support beyond Förster's fourteenth-century linguistic estimate?

next_steps: Ask the Bodleian to confirm current foliation and the exact first and last Middle English lines.
Reconcile the locators against a complete facsimile when obtained without deleting the historical claims.
openQuestions.question: How do the three Rawlinson boundaries—Förster/CME ff. 1r–11v, DIMEV ff. 1–13, and the current catalog's inferred ff. 1r–16v item—map to the physical text?

next_steps: Locate a modern critical edition or textual study of the Northern Cato.
Treat Förster's and Brusendorff's judgments as attributed analyses rather than direct-copy relations among extant manuscripts.
openQuestions.question: What current stemma best explains the Thott, Sidney, and strongly revised Rawlinson versions?

assertion.notes: Förster bases this on rhymes, morphology, and Scandinavian vocabulary; DIMEV retains 'Northern' in its work description.
Förster calls Northern England the place of origin, but dialect evidence alone is not independent documentary proof of where the translation was made.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous translator-adapter of the Northern Middle English Cato
notes: The translation is a free verse adaptation: normally two, sometimes four or five, rhyme pairs render one Latin distich.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous translator-adapter of the Northern Middle English Cato

scope.description: Immediate 1906 source edition represented by CME00083

subjects.label: Moral advice and conduct of life

summaries.value: An anonymous Northern Middle English verse translation and adaptation of the Latin Disticha Catonis, probably made in the fourteenth century. A father addresses a son with practical and moral instruction on virtue, speech, friendship, work, wealth, adversity, self-command, and conduct. The work survives in three divergent witnesses: the compact Thott manuscript, which interleaves each Latin distich with its English rendering; the Sidney Sussex copy following Confessio Amantis; and a strongly revised Rawlinson version. Förster's 1906 edition prints the Sidney and Rawlinson texts separately on alternating pages, while CME00083 serializes them as two consecutive streams.

The 297 LG and 1,265 L elements in CME00083 aggregate two representations of one work: Sidney has 153 LG and 654 L; Rawlinson has 144 LG and 611 L. These are encoding metrics, not an authored extent of 297 sequential stanzas or 1,265 lines. The unencoded Thott witness is the third witness in the current DIMEV census.

assertion.notes: A normalized discovery title based on DIMEV's description and Förster's established title; no authorial medieval title is known.

A father addresses a son with practical and moral instruction on virtue, speech, friendship, work, wealth, adversity, self-command, and conduct. The work survives in three divergent witnesses: the compact Thott manuscript, which interleaves each Latin distich with its English rendering; the Sidney Sussex copy following Confessio Amantis; and a strongly revised Rawlinson version.
